title: Cat Chronic Diarrhea with Mucus: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discover causes for chronic diarrhea with mucus in cats, learn when to seek veterinary care, and find out why stool photos assist in diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Chronic diarrhea with mucus in cats usually indicates large intestine inflammation (colitis) and requires a veterinary appointment within 24–48 hours, unless accompanied by severe vomiting or lethargy.

What causes chronic diarrhea with mucus in cats?

Chronic diarrhea is defined as loose or liquid stools that persist for more than three weeks. When you observe mucus—a slimy, jelly-like substance—in your cat's stool, it typically indicates inflammation in the large intestine (colitis). The colon produces this mucus to protect its lining from irritation caused by various factors such as parasites, food sensitivities, or inflammatory diseases.

When should I seek emergency veterinary care for my cat's diarrhea?

  • Urgency Level: Medium.
  • While chronic diarrhea is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it indicates an underlying health issue that requires a consultation.
  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat exhibits signs of extreme lethargy, persistent vomiting, or a refusal to drink water to prevent severe dehydration.

How does a photo of my cat's stool help the veterinarian with triage?

  • A visual record allows the vet to assess the exact color, consistency, and the specific distribution of mucus or blood.
  • These details help determine if the issue originates in the small or large intestine, narrowing down potential causes.
  • Clear photos help guide initial diagnostic tests, such as fecal exams or blood work, more efficiently.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, chronic colitis commonly manifests as large-bowel diarrhea, characterized by mucus, hematochezia, tenesmus, and occasional pain during defecation. Affected cats often exhibit increased urgency and frequency of defecation, producing decreased fecal volume per bowel movement; weight loss and vomiting are uncommon unless the small intestine is also involved. Initial diagnostics should include a complete history, physical examination with rectal palpation, and fecal evaluation for parasites (e.g., Tritrichomonas foetus), bacteria (e.g., Clostridium), and fungal elements (e.g., Histoplasma capsulatum); rectal cytology can help exclude other causes of large-bowel diarrhea. Suspected Clostridium perfringens colitis (>5 endospores per field) should be confirmed by identifying enterotoxins A and B via ELISA after fecal bacterial culture. Feline leukemia virus/feline immunodeficiency virus testing, as well as a thyroid level if age-appropriate, are recommended, along with a dietary trial, before pursuing more advanced diagnostics. If clinical signs persist, a CBC, biochemical profile, and urinalysis should be performed to exclude other diseases.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 378)

faqs.question: What are common causes of mucus in a cat's stool?
faqs.answer: Common causes include intestinal parasites (such as Giardia or Tritrichomonas foetus), dietary indiscretion, food allergies, or chronic conditions like Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD).

faqs.question: Should I change my cat's food immediately?
faqs.answer: It is best to wait for veterinary advice. Sudden diet changes can often worsen diarrhea. Your vet may recommend a specific therapeutic diet once they have determined the cause of the inflammation.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat over-the-counter human diarrhea medication?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give human medications to cats without specific instructions from a veterinarian, as many are toxic to felines and can lead to serious complications.
